tour.title: Project Overview
tour.description: Start with README.md to understand MindForge's purpose as a sovereign agentic intelligence framework for Claude Code. This document outlines the installation process, core workflows, and architectural vision — setting the stage for exploring the multi-agent orchestration system that follows.

tour.title: Installation Entry Point
tour.description: The install.js CLI entry orchestrates the entire setup process, from wizard-driven configuration to dependency installation and verification. This is where users first interact with MindForge, establishing the framework's runtime environment and preparing the autonomous execution pipeline.

tour.title: Identity & Behavioral OS
tour.description: SOUL.md defines MindForge's sovereign identity — the behavioral operating system that governs decision-making, success metrics, and core truths. MINDFORGE.md serves as the parameter registry, declaring intelligence toggles, model topology, governance rules, and resource constraints. Together, these documents establish the 'why' and 'what' before the code implements the 'how'.

tour.title: Installation Orchestrator
tour.description: The installer-core.js module coordinates the setup wizard, migration execution, and post-install verification. Building on install.js from Step 2, this module implements the actual installation logic, ensuring configuration files are written, dependencies are resolved, and the framework state is initialized correctly.

tour.title: Central Observability Engine
tour.description: Nexus Tracer is MindForge's distributed tracing backbone, recording reasoning chains, generating SBOM artifacts, calculating entropy, and integrating with secure enclaves. Every autonomous action, policy decision, and memory operation flows through this observability layer, making it the single source of truth for governance and debugging.
tour.languageLesson: JavaScript singletons in Node.js use a module-level variable to ensure only one instance exists across all imports. The Nexus Tracer maintains global state like trace ID, session context, and audit log handles, making it accessible from any layer without explicit dependency injection.

tour.title: Memory Foundation
tour.description: The vector-hub.js (SQLite-backed vector database) and knowledge-graph.js (weighted edge store) form MindForge's persistent memory layer. Vector-hub provides FTS search, trace recording, and migration tracking, while knowledge-graph maintains relationships between entities with decay and reinforcement. These systems underpin context retention, semantic search, and multi-session continuity.
tour.languageLesson: SQLite is embedded directly into the Node.js process, requiring no separate database server. This makes it ideal for CLI tools and local-first architectures where simplicity and zero-config deployment matter more than horizontal scalability.

tour.title: Autonomous Execution Engine
tour.description: The auto-runner.js orchestrator implements MindForge's autonomous execution loop with multi-wave task execution, repair operators, and stuck detection. It coordinates with the Nexus Tracer (Step 5) for observability and the memory systems (Step 6) for context retrieval, enabling self-healing workflows that recover from failures without human intervention.

tour.title: Multi-LLM Orchestration
tour.description: The model-client.js facade abstracts Anthropic, OpenAI, and Gemini providers behind a unified interface, while model-router.js selects the optimal model based on task complexity and MINDFORGE.md configuration. This layer enables cost-aware routing, cross-provider failover, and seamless model switching without rewriting application logic.
tour.languageLesson: Facade pattern consolidates multiple provider SDKs into a single interface. Instead of application code importing @anthropic-ai/sdk, openai, and @google-ai/generativelanguage directly, it uses ModelClient.generate() which delegates to the appropriate provider based on routing rules.

tour.title: Governance & Policy
tour.description: The policy-engine.js enforces multi-tier governance rules with ZK-proof verification and comprehensive audit logging. Policies control access to sensitive operations, validate autonomous decisions, and ensure compliance with the behavioral constraints defined in SOUL.md (Step 3). Every action subject to governance is traced by Nexus (Step 5) and logged immutably.

tour.title: CLI Command Router
tour.description: The mindforge-tools.cjs aggregates all CLI commands (like /mindforge:next, /mindforge:auto, /mindforge:status) and provides unified routing. This is the bridge between the Claude Code interface and the framework internals explored in Steps 5-9, dispatching user commands to the appropriate execution engines.

tour.title: Dashboard & Visualization
tour.description: The dashboard server (HTTP on port 7339) exposes real-time metrics, temporal sliders, and AgRevOps governance data via SSE and REST endpoints. The metrics-aggregator.js consolidates data from Nexus Tracer, memory systems, and autonomous execution logs, providing operators with visibility into cost, velocity, and security health scores.

tour.title: CI/CD & Automation
tour.description: The AI Intelligence GitHub Actions workflow automates quality gates, security scans, and deployment pipelines. This pipeline leverages the same autonomous execution engine (Step 7) and policy enforcement (Step 9) used in local development, ensuring consistency between local validation and production deployment.
tour.languageLesson: GitHub Actions workflows define jobs with dependencies using needs: [job-id]. Matrix builds parallelize tests across Node versions. Secrets are injected via ${{ secrets.NAME }} and never logged. Actions cache node_modules between runs to speed up CI by avoiding redundant npm install.

tour.title: Configuration & Package Manifest
tour.description: The package.json declares MindForge's dependencies, version, and CLI entry points (bin/install.js from Step 2). Configuration files like settings.json define runtime behavior, plugin activation, and model topology. These files are the declarative layer that the installation orchestrator (Step 4) writes and the CLI router (Step 10) reads.
